feed.title: Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge

entry.title: Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge
entry.summary:
Luke Childs' recent optimization efforts have successfully reduced the size of a cryptographic proof from 1.8 MB to 200 KB by using a recursion layer technique. While this method has significantly decreased the data size, it has increased the proof time from 50 seconds to approximately 180 seconds. This technique works by initially generating the main proof followed by an additional proof that verifies the first, thus allowing for a high complexity proof to be compressed into a statement that maintains a constant size despite its complexity.
Further development details and specific changes made can be referenced through commits on GitHub. The first series of updates is available at [this link](https://github.com/Roasbeef/go-zkvm/compare/f691458110b6b551a20ab36d15de98dfb6813d8c..77420055510acfc2f0bee2dcf2313365dee927e8), while subsequent modifications can be viewed at [another link](https://github.com/Roasbeef/bip32-pq-zkp/compare/aae0a1a763eed6bfab8660bcfb8f938f91b10c10..4dcb731e4fc97f84e52c85d2768dc41612c71027). Additionally, Osuntokun has shared sketch notes concerning the construction of an aggregated proof via a Merkle tree composition, which aims to enable a singular proof within a block for multiple statements. These notes are accessible [here](https://github.com/Roasbeef/bip32-pq-zkp/blob/main/docs/claim.mdfuture-recursive-composition-sketch).
These advancements reflect significant progress in optimizing blockchain technology's efficiency and scalability, particularly in areas requiring compact yet complex proofs. Such innovations not only enhance performance but also potentially lower operational costs by reducing the space required for proof storage and validation on the blockchain.
